coredata.dc:title: Novel carbon quantum dot modified g-C3N4 nanotubes on carbon cloth for efficient degradation of ciprofloxacin
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yue
Li, Xuefei
Lei, Weiwei
Zhu, Bolin
Yang, Jinghai
coredata.dc:description: The dimensionality of materials plays an important role in the photocatalytic applications. Herein, we report a novel photocatalyst by implanting zero-dimensional carbon quantum dots (CQDs) within one-dimensional porous tubular graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) on carbon cloth (CC). The degradation of ciprofloxacin under visible light irradiation can be 98% obtained within 60 min by the photocatalyst (g-C3N4/CQD/CC), due to the improvement of light absorption and reduction of rapid recombination of photogenerated carriers of the g-C3N4/CQD micro-regional heterojunction. In addition, density functional theory (DFT) calculation and a set of characterisation were applied to study the influence of implanted CQDs on the band gap and charge behavior of g-C3N4/CQD/CC. Moreover, a novel multilayer photocatalytic reactor was designed to simulate the degradation capability of g-C3N4/CQD/CC by flowing wastewater, which exhibits excellent photocatalysis performance. The introduction of a CC substrate can improve the separation and cycle life of this new photocatalyst.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalyst
Carbon quantum dots
Tubular g-C3N4
g-C3N4/CQD/CC
Carbon cloth

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 61705020), the Development of Science and Technology of Jilin Province (No. 20180101206JC, 20190103002JH), Graduate Education Innovation Program of Jilin Normal University (No. 202010) and the Australian Research Council Discovery Program (DP190103290).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the fabrication of the sample (g-C3N4/CQD/CC).
TEM image, high-resolution TEM image (a) and diameter distribution of CQDs.
SEM images of (a) CC, (b) ZnO/CC, (c) g-C3N4/CC, and (d) g-C3N4/CQD/CC.
TEM image (a) and high-resolution TEM image (b) of g-C3N4/CQD/CC, SEM image of g-C3N4/CC (c), SEM image of ZnO/CC (f) and EDS elemental mappings of C (d), N (e), Zn (g) and O (h).

XRD patterns (a) of CC, ZnO/CC, g-C3N4/CC and g-C3N4/CQD/CC, FT-IR spectra of g-C3N4/CC and g-C3N4/CQD/CC, XPS survey (c), high-resolution (d) C 1 s and N 1 s XPS spectra of g-C3N4/CC and g-C3N4/CQD/CC and ratios of N1/(N2 + N3) relative contents for different types of N 1 s (f).
UV − vis diffuse reflectance spectra (a), band gap energies (b), VB XPS spectra (c and e) and UPS spectra (d and f) of g-C3N4/CC and g-C3N4/CQD/CC.
Optimised unit cells (a and b) and calculated density of states (c and d) for g-C3N4/CC and g-C3N4/CQD/CC.
(a) Photoinduced i-t curves over different photocatalysis under visible light irradiation and (b) EIS nyquist plots of g-C3N4/CC and g-C3N4/CQD/CC.

Photocatalytic activity (a and b) of CC, g-C3N4/CC and g-C3N4/CQD/CC, degradation cycling experiments under visible light (c) and photodegradation rates of ciprofloxacin over g-C3N4/CQD/CC in the presence of different scavengers (d).
(a) Schematic diagram of a multi-layered photocatalytic reactor, (b) The plot of degradation efficiency of ciprofloxacin by CC and g-C3N4/CQD/CC versus the layered numbers of photocatalytic reactor.
Schematic illustration for the mechanism of photoinduced charge carrier transfers in g-C3N4/CQD/CC under visible light irradiation.
Fitted EIS data: g-C3N4/CC and g-C3N4/CQD/CC.

head.ce:title: Novel carbon quantum dot modified g-C3N4 nanotubes on carbon cloth for efficient degradation of ciprofloxacin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Physics and Chemistry of the Ministry of Education, Jilin Normal University, Changchun 130000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Physics and Chemistry of the Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Physics and Chemistry of the Ministry of Education, Jilin Normal University, Changchun 130000, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Frontier Materials, Deakin University, Waurn Ponds Campus, Locked Bag 20000, Victoria 3220, Australia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Frontier Materials, Deakin University, Waurn Ponds Campus, Locked Bag 20000, Victoria 3220, Australia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The dimensionality of materials plays an important role in the photocatalytic applications. Herein, we report a novel photocatalyst by implanting zero-dimensional carbon quantum dots (CQDs) within one-dimensional porous tubular graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) on carbon cloth (CC). The degradation of ciprofloxacin under visible light irradiation can be 98% obtained within 60 min by the photocatalyst (g-C3N4/CQD/CC), due to the improvement of light absorption and reduction of rapid recombination of photogenerated carriers of the g-C3N4/CQD micro-regional heterojunction. In addition, density functional theory (DFT) calculation and a set of characterisation were applied to study the influence of implanted CQDs on the band gap and charge behavior of g-C3N4/CQD/CC. Moreover, a novel multilayer photocatalytic reactor was designed to simulate the degradation capability of g-C3N4/CQD/CC by flowing wastewater, which exhibits excellent photocatalysis performance. The introduction of a CC substrate can improve the separation and cycle life of this new photocatalyst.

ce:section.ce:para: Currently, environmental issues, expecially water pollutions, have attracted increasing amounts of research effort. Photocatalytic reactions, driven by solar-energy, is a potentially approach for resolving these problems [1–3]. Recently, metal-free polymeric g-C3N4 as a catalyst has become a focus of research for effectively degrading the environmental pollutants [2,4–6]. Compared to other photocatalysts, g-C3N4 has been used on account of its band gap of 2.7 eV, low cost of production, and high chemical stability. However, the g-C3N4 has low specific surface area and high photogenic carrier recombination efficiency, both of which are not conducive to photocatalytic reactions [7–11]. In recent years, g-C3N4 photocatalysts with different morphologies have been investigated [12–15]. Because their morphology and dimensions can affect the performance of photocatalysis due to the confinement of the nanostructure to electrons [16–21]. However, most special morphologies of g-C3N4 are prepared by complex and costly methods [13,22,23]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a high-active photocatalyst at low cost, with simple operation and a special nanostructure. The construction of heterojunctions between g-C3N4 and other materials is also an effective method to improve the catalytic activity. CQDs have inspired great interest due to their physico-chemical properties, such as low toxicity, excellent optical properties, and electron-mediating ability [24–28]. It has been reported that the activity of a photocatalyst was improved by the unique properties of CQDs [29–31]. Therefore, the photocatalysts with heterojunction structures of CQDs can enhance the separation efficiency of photogenic electron and hole pairs and improve their ability to absorb visible light [32].

In addition, the recyclability of photocatalysts is another issue in practice, resulting in waste and secondary pollution. Therefore, it is necessary to introduce a suitable photocatalyst carrier to resolve this issue. Carbon cloth (CC) is used as a substrate for making novel nanostructures due to its corrosion resistance, flexibility, and large surface area [33,34]. We have previously reported the construction of NiCo2O4/g-C3N4 heterojunction on the carbon cloth for photocatalytic applications [35]. However, the growth of metal-free photocatalyst carbon quantum dots modified g-C3N4 on carbon cloth has not been reported.

In this work, we synthesised g-C3N4 nanotubes on CC using zinc oxide self-sacrificial template, and then micro-regional heterojunctions of g-C3N4/CQD were constructed. The photocatalytic degradation of g-C3N4/CQD/CC for ciprofloxacin was evaluated under visible light irradiation. To explain the improved photocatalytic activity, the possible mechanism was discussed. The introduction of a CC substrate is beneficial to the separation and recycling of the photocatalyst. Importantly, no significant decrease in photocatalytic activity was observed after repeated use.

Monohydrate citric acid (CA), 1, 2, 4-benzeneetriamine dihydrochloride (BD), urea (CH4N2O), zinc acetate ((CH3COO)2Zn), carbon cloth (CC), zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)·6(H2O)), hexamethylenetetramine (C6H12N4), acetone (CH3COCH3), methanol (CH3OH), anhydrous ethanol (C2H6O) and deionised water (DW).

1 mol CA and 1 mol BD were dissolved in 10 ml deionised water. Then it was stirred to form a transparent solution. The transparent solution was transferred into a 30 ml autoclave and heated at 300 °C for 6 h. The resulting CQDs were dialysed for 24 h in deionised water with the molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) dialysis bag rated at 3000 Da and then further dialysed for 12 h in deionised water using a MWCO of 3500 Da. Finally, the CQDs were obtained and washed with DW and C2H6O, then dried.

Firstly, CC (30 mm × 30 mm) was washed in the mixture of CH3COCH3 (10 ml), C2H6O (10 ml) and DW (10 ml) by ultrasonicating for 15 min. Then, it was dried in the oven at 60 °C for 10 h. The CC was soaked in a mixture of 0.001 mol (CH3COO)2Zn and 50 ml CH3OH for 1 h. After being removed, it was heated at 300 °C for 20 min. The pre-processed CC was soaked mixture of Zn(NO3)·6(H2O) (0.1 mol L−1) and C6H12N4 (0.1 mol L−1) and heated at 90 °C for 4 h. Finally, the coated CC was removed, cleaned in DW and C2H6O. Then it was dried in the oven at 60 °C for 10 h.

The ZnO/CC (30 mm × 30 mm) was soaked in a mixture of 1 mol CH4N2O and 50 ml DW for 1 h. Then, the pre-treated ZnO/CC was transfered and heated at 60 °C for 1.5 h. After that, the pre-treated ZnO/CC was covered with CH4N2O powder and heated 550 °C for 4 h in a crucible. In the end, the pre-treated CC was washed in C2H6O for removing the g-C 3 N 4 powder which is easy to peel off. Then it was dried in the oven at 60 °C for 10 h. The load of g-C3N4 on the CC was about 2 mg/cm2.

The ZnO/CC (30 mm × 30 mm) was soaked in a mixture of 1 mol CH4N2O and 50 ml DW. The pre-treated ZnO/CC was transfered and heated at 60 °C for 1.5 h. The as-coated ZnO/CC was mixed with urea power with adding 5 ml CQD dispersion (1 mg/ml). This was then heated to 550 °C for 4 h. In the end, the pre-treated CC was washed in C2H6O for removing the g-C3N4 powder which is easy to peel off. Then it was dried in the oven at 60 °C for 10 h. The load of g-C3N4/CQD on the CC was about 2.1 mg/cm2.
More details about the experimental section are demonstrated in Supplementary Material.

The preparation process of g-C3N4/CQD/CC was shown in Fig. 1 . The TEM image and high-resolution TEM image of CQDs are shown in Fig. 2 a. As shown in the inset of Fig. 2a, the lattice fringe spacing is 0.22 nm, which corresponds to (100) lattice plane of graphite [36]. In the Fig. 2b, the size distribution of CQDs is in the range from 1.85 to 7.65 nm. The morphologies of the samples are shown in Fig. 3 . The original CC was black, and SEM images reveal that they have a smooth surface (Fig. 3a). Self-sacrificing template ZnO nanorods (diameter: 80 nm) were grown in-situ on CC by hydrothermal methods (Fig. 3b). Porous tubular g-C3N4 with a diameter of 100 nm and a wall thickness of 10 nm can be seen in Fig. 3c and d. The distribution of hollow g-C3N4 nanotubes in CC was observed as well. In Fig. 3d, there are no obvious nanoparticles grown on the smooth surface of hollow g-C3N4 nanotubes of g-C3N4/CQD/CC due to the small size of CQDs. In addition, the above morphologies of C3N4/CC and C3N4/CQD/CC were also confirmed in AFM (Fig. S1).

The TEM image of g-C3N4/CQD/CC is shown in Fig. 4 a. The diameter and wall thickness of porous nanotube-like g-C3N4 are 100 nm and 10 nm, respectively, which are consistent with the SEM results above. Many CQDs are seen on the constitutive g-C3N4, resulting in a microscopic heterojunction between CQD and g-C3N4. In Fig. 4b, the lattice fringe spacing of CQDs is the same as lattice fringe spacing in Fig. 2a. In Fig. 4d and e, the elemental C and N are homogeneously distributed in the fibres of CC in images of elemental mappings for g-C3N4/CQD/CC. The SEM image and elemental mappings of ZnO/CC are shown in Fig. 3f to h.

XRD patterns of CC, ZnO/CC, g-C3N4/CC and g-C3N4/CQD/CC are shown in Fig. 5 a. There are no obvious diffraction peaks of ZnO found in g-C3N4/CC, indicating that the self-sacrificial template of ZnO was completely removed [37]. During the preparation of g-C3N4/CC, ZnO nanorods were reduced to Zn [38,39]. Finally, Zn evaporated with the gas flow, leaving tubular g-C3N4 [40]. The peaks at 27.43 and 13.12, corresponding to lattice plane (002 and 100), respectively, these are characteristic peaks of g-C3N4 in g-C3N4/CC and g-C3N4/CQD/CC. The diffraction peaks of g-C3N4/CC and g-C3N4/CQD/CC are similar, indicating that the CQDs did not change the g-C3N4 crystal structure [27]. There are no new characteristic peaks observed for the g-C3N4/CQD/CC. This may be due to the fact that CQDs is particularly uniformly dispersed, and the amount of CQDs is too small relative to g-C3N4. In order to weaken the influence of other factors such as carbon cloth substrate on XRD diffraction peak, the XRD results of g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/CQD powder are shown in the Fig. S2. As a result, the (002) diffraction peak from g-C3N4 clearly indicates a shift. This crystal lattice distortion further demonstrates the introduction of CQDs in g-C3N4. Fig. 5b shows the FTIR spectra of g-C3N4/CC and g-C3N4/CQD/CC. Several absorption peaks of C-N stretching vibrations in the CN heterocycles were observed at 1000–1700 cm−1 [10]. The broad peak at 3000–3300 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibration of N-H, and the peak at 810 cm−1 can be attributed to the breathing mode of triazine units [41]. It was found that g-C3N4/CC and g-C3N4/CQD/CC are identical in the FTIR spectrum, indicating that the structure of g-C3N4 is not changed by the microscopic heterojunctions formed by CQD implantation [29]. That is also confirmed by the Raman spectrum (Fig. S3).

The XPS survey spectra of g-C3N4/CC and g-C3N4/CQD/CC are shown in Fig. 5c-e. No peaks of ZnO are found in Fig. 4c, which further confirms that the ZnO template is completely removed. Two peaks of C1s at 288.2 eV and 284.8 eV in Fig. 5d correspond to C=C and sp2-hybridized carbon (N = C-N2) binding energy in Fig. 4d, respectively. The peaks at 399.0 eV, 400.4 eV, and 401.6 eV correspond to sp2-hybridised nitrogen (C=N-C: N1), bridging nitrogen (C)3-N: N2 or N3), and amino groups carrying hydrogen (N-H) binding energy, respectively, as seen in Fig. 5e. The g-C3N4/CQD/CC (4.697) has a higher ratio (N1/(N2 + N3)) than g-C3N4/CC (1.201), implying that the g-C3N4/CQD/CC has more C=N-C groups (Fig. 5f). The high ratio (N1/(N2 + N3)) also indicates the formation of heterogeneous junction between CQD and g-C3N4 in Fig. 6
[42].

The samples of g-C3N4/CC and g-C3N4/CQD/CC were measured by UV-vis spectra (Fig. 7 a). The loading of CQD into the porous tubular g-C3N4 enhanced the visible wavelength range. This indicates that the CQD improved the light absorption capacity of g-C3N4/CQD/CC. As a result, more photons can be absorbed to generate more electron hole pairs, thus increasing the photocatalytic activity [15,43]. The band gap energies of g-C3N4/CC and g-C3N4/CQD/CC are 2.90 and 2.85 eV (Fig. 7b). The bands of the g-C3N4/CC and g-C3N4/CQD/CC photocatalysts at 2.03 and 1.83 eV correspond to their Fermi levels (E F) [44]. The work functions of g-C3N4/CC (4.42 eV) and g-C3N4/CQD/CC (5.22 eV) photocatalysts are shown in Fig. 7d and f [45]. Then combining the band gap energies of the photocatalyst, the values of the conduction band (CB) of g-C3N4/CC and g-C3N4/CQD/CC are 3.55 and 4.20 eV, and the valence bands (VB) of the g-C3N4/CC and g-C3N4/CQD/CC are 6.45 and 7.05 eV.

The effect of CQD on carrier behaviour of g-C3N4/CQD/CC was revealed through the DFT calculation. The optimised unit structures of g-C3N4/CC and g-C3N4/CQD/CC are shown in Fig. 8 a and b. Due to the limitations of DFT calculation, the calculated values of g-C3N4/CC (2.45 eV) and g-C3N4/CQD/CC (0.45 eV) are less than the experimental values, however, these results still confirm that CQD-modified g-C3N4/CQD/CC has a narrower band gap than g-C3N4/ CC. In the electron density of states (Fig. 8c) for g-C3N4/CC, the Fermi level of g-C3N4/CC is in the middle of the band gap. In Fig. 7d, it can be observed that electron density distributes around the Fermi level. The electron properties of this Fermi level between mobile electrons will greatly reduce the interfacial Schottky barrier of g-C3N4/CQD/CC in photocatalysis [42].

The transfer behaviours of photogenic carriers were evaluated by photocurrent experiments in Fig. 9 a. Compared with g-C3N4/CC, the photocurrent intensity of g-C3N4/CQD/CC significantly increases, demonstrating that the efficiency of charge separation and transfer is enhanced. The g-C3N4/CQD/CC shows a much smaller arc radius in Fig. 9b. The corresponding fitting parameters of series resistance (R s), transfer resistance (R tr) and recombination resistance (R rec) are presented in Table 1
[46]. These results indicate the g-C3N4/CQD/CC has a lower interfacial resistance with higher photocatalytic activity [12,29].

The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of g-C3N4/CC and g-C3N4/CQD/CC are illustrated in Fig. S4. The surface areas of g-C3N4/CC (24.4 m2/g) and g-C3N4/CQD/CC (24.5 m2/g) are determined. The introduction of CQDs does not lead to a significant increase in the surface area of g-C3N4/CQD/CC, therefore, the increase of surface area of g-C3N4/CQD/CC photocatalyst relative to g-C3N4/CC has little effect on photocatalytic activity.

The degradation curves of ciprofloxacin are shown in Fig. 10 a. Different photocatalysts (CC, g-C3N4/CC and g-C3N4/CQD/CC) were added to a pollutant solution (ciprofloxacin: 30 ml, 5 mg/L) brought to adsorption equilibrium after resting in the dark for 30 min. In Fig. 10a, bare CC substrates show little degradation of ciprofloxacin. Compared with others, g-C3N4/CQD/CC shows the highest photocatalytic activity in terms of degradation of ciprofloxacin. This is because the heterojunction improves the absorptive capacity of visible light in the photocatalyst and inhibits the rapid recombination of photogenic electron-hole pairs. In addition, CQDs modified by tubular g-C3N4 surface can increase the number of photocatalytic active sites as well [2]. The photocatalyst of g-C3N4/CQD/CC also shows good stability in the cyclic experiment (Fig. 10c).

In this work, a novel photocatalytic reactor was designed to research the potential practical applications of our novel photocatalyst (Fig. 11 a). The flowing wastewater (ciprofloxacin: 5 mg/L, 0.5 L/h) flows from the lower end of the photocatalytic reactor under the irradiation of visible light and passes through multiple layers of photocatalyst. The structure of the multi-layer photocatalytic reactor is conducive to accelerating the degradation of pollutants. In Fig. 11b, there are negligible changes in the concentrations of pollutants passing through CC under dark and light conditions. In contrast, the g-C3N4/CQD/CC shows excellent photocatalytic activity under light conditions. The degradation efficiency of pollutant of g-C3N4/CQD/CC can reach 99%, indicating that it has great potential in the practical application of the degradation of organic waste water. As shown in the illustration in Fig. 11b, the g-C3N4/CQD/CC shows good flexibility as well.

To further study the degradation mechanism of ciprofloxacin, radical trapping experiments of g-C3N4/CQD/CC were conducted (Fig. 10d). Isopropyl alcohol (IPA), benzoquinone (BQ), and trolamine (TEOA) are quenchers of hydroxyl radicals (·OH), superoxide radical anion (·O2−) and holes (h+), respectively [29]. Obviously, ·O2− is the main active substance, while ·OH and h+ have a little effect [1,12,15].

From what has been discussed above, the reasonable photocatalytic mechanism is as shown in Fig. 12 . From band gap energy, it can be concluded that g-C3N4 is active in visible light. When the catalyst is irradiated by visible light, the VB electrons of g-C3N4 are excited to their respective CB. CQD was implanted with tubular g-C3N4, which allows g-C3N4/CQD/CC to absorb more photons under visible light, thus generating more photogenerated carriers. Because of the construction of a heterojunction, the separation efficiency of photogenic electrons and holes is improved. It can effectively prevent the recombination of photogenerated carriers improving the activity of photocatalyst and showing better photodegradation efficiency. That provides a rapid transfer of photogenic electrons on the CB of g-C3N4, and meanwhile holes are produced on the VB of g-C3N4. The ·O2 – radicals were generated by the oxygen reaction of electrons on the CB and holes will react with OH– product ·OH [15,32]. Totally, the ·O2– radicals, photogenic holes, and ·OH can break down ciprofloxacin into small CO2 and H2O molecules [43].

In conclusion, CQD-modified porous tubular g-C3N4 was successfully grown on CC. In visible light, g-C3N4/CQD/CC shows enhanced photocatalytic activity in the degradation of ciprofloxacin. Through a series of characterization and photocatalytic activity studies, it was confirmed that the introduction of CQD played a key role in the improvement of photocatalytic activity. The multi-layer photocatalytic reactor was constructed to confirm that g-C3N4/CQD/CC had a high activity of degrading flowed ciprofloxacin. This strategy is of great significance when trying to improve the activity and recovery of photocatalysts. This also improves the effectiveness of photocatalysts in practical applications, especially in the degradation of organic pollutants in the flowing wastewater.

Yue Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Visualization. Xuefei Li: Software, Supervision, Formal analysis, Resources, Funding acquisition. Weiwei Lei: Software, Supervision, Formal analysis, Resources, Funding acquisition. Bolin Zhu: Software. Jinghai Yang: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 61705020), the Development of Science and Technology of Jilin Province (No. 20180101206JC, 20190103002JH), Graduate Education Innovation Program of Jilin Normal University (No. 202010) and the Australian Research Council Discovery Program (DP190103290).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2021.149967.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: RGO-MoS2 supported NiCo2O4 catalyst towards solar water splitting and dye degradation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Chakrabarty, A. Mukherjee, S. Basu, RGO-MoS2 supported NiCo2O4 catalyst towards solar water splitting and dye degradation, ACS Sustainable Chemistry & Engineering, 6 (2018) 5238-5247.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A study of spherical TiO2/g-C3N4 photocatalyst: Morphology, chemical composition and photocatalytic performance in visible light

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Jiang, X. Yang, Y. Wu, Z. Li, Y. Han, X. Shen, A study of spherical TiO2 /g-C3N4 photocatalyst: Morphology, chemical composition and photocatalytic performance in visible light, Molecular Catalysis, 432 (2017) 232-241.
J. Wen, J. Xie, X. Chen, X. Li, A review on g-C3N4-based photocatalysts, Applied Surface Science, 391 (2017) 72-123.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Porous graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets prepared under self-producing atmosphere for highly improved photocatalytic activity

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Song, Q. Yang, X. Jiang, M. Yin, L. Zhou, Porous graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets prepared under self-producing atmosphere for highly improved photocatalytic activity, Applied Catalysis B: Environmental, 217 (2017) 322-330.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Polymeric graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4)-based semiconducting nanostructured materials: Synthesis methods, properties and photocatalytic applications

Aminabhavi, Polymeric graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4)-based semiconducting nanostructured materials: Synthesis methods, properties and photocatalytic applications, Journal of Environmental Management, 238 (2019) 25-40.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene/graphitic carbon nitride-based ternary nanohybrids: Synthesis methods, properties, and applications for photocatalytic hydrogen production

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Singla, S. Sharma, S. Basu, N.P. Shetti, K.R. Reddy, Graphene/graphitic carbon nitride-based ternary nanohybrids: Synthesis methods, properties, and applications for photocatalytic hydrogen production, FlatChem, 24 (2020) 100200.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4)-based metal-free photocatalysts for water splitting: A review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Mishra, A. Mehta, S. Basu, N.P. Shetti, K.R. Reddy, T.M. Aminabhavi, Graphitic carbon nitride (g–C3N4)–based metal-free photocatalysts for water splitting: A review, Carbon, 149 (2019) 693-721.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electro-sensing base for herbicide aclonifen at graphitic carbon nitride modified carbon electrode-Water and soil sample analysis
